_comment: The driver-fatigue-management (LOG-01) eval set. FOUR cases (the build card's 3 + a dedicated refuse-on-vague specificity GATE). Case 1 is the CORE-VALUE lever — schedule/roster redesign first: a treatment that leads with 'stay alert' driver-alertness training / an in-cab fatigue-detection gadget for a roster that forces an HOS break breach must be FLAGGED as alertness-led and pushed up the hierarchy (roster / journey-plan / built-in-rest FRMS redesign first); the HOS compliance flags are COMPUTED by fatigue.py (fmcsa_compliance / eu561_compliance), never narrated, and are the AUTHORITATIVE finding, while the fatigue index is rendered as a clearly-flagged ADVISORY metric (advisory: True), never a regulatory threshold. Case 1 carries the CANDIDATE golden output evals/output/driver-fatigue-management.md. Case 2 is the multi-day [GAP] discipline — a single-shift duty log cannot evidence the FMCSA 60/70 h cycle, the 34 h restart, or the EU 561 weekly/fortnightly driving and cross-shift daily rest; the skill records a literal [GAP] and NEVER asserts the cycle/restart/weekly compliant from one shift, and never invents a driving hour or rest segment. Case 3 is the de-id PAIR (highest occupational-health tier): the eval CASE wires the CLEAN positive (evals/files/driver-fatigue-management-clean.md) so the per-skill gate passes without a spurious hard-fail; the SEEDED-LEAK NEGATIVE (evals/files/driver-fatigue-management-leak.md) is proven CAUGHT by the deterministic de-id grader (auto_fail True on a named driver + CDL/phone + an OSA/sleep-disorder note + a <5 fatigue-event cell + an embedded re-id key, including the <5 small-cell suppression HARD-fail), while the paired clean version does NOT trip. Case 4 is the specificity GATE: refuse to 'manage our fatigue' / refuse a 'just run a stay-alert toolbox talk' treatment until the named fleet/operation + the jurisdiction + the driver duty log are captured. The roster-redesign-first hierarchy gate is a deterministic controls.py call (rank_controls / validate_treatment) and the HOS compliance is a deterministic fatigue.py call — never a narrated judgment. rubric.yaml is the canonical template/evals/rubric.yaml. Model-graded weighted >=4.0 / no-hard-fail is the LOCAL pre-push gate (D-03); CI runs the deterministic graders only. The golden output is a CANDIDATE — owner LOCKS in P17.

evals.query: Review the night-trunk route R-204 (Midlands depot) for driver fatigue. Here is one driver's shift duty log: 5.0 h driving, then 3.5 h driving, then 11 h off-duty (EU 561 / GB drivers' hours). The depot's proposed fix is 'just run a stay-alert toolbox talk and fit in-cab drowsiness alarms — the drivers need to stay sharper'. Produce the driver-fatigue-management assessment.
evals.expected_behavior: The HOS compliance flags are COMPUTED by fatigue.py (eu561_compliance) from the duty log, never narrated: the 45-min break after 4.5 h continuous driving (Art. 7) is a FAIL (5.0 h continuous with no qualifying break), and this is the authoritative finding. The fatigue index is rendered as a clearly-flagged ADVISORY metric (advisory: True), never a regulatory threshold. The depot's 'stay-alert toolbox talk + in-cab drowsiness alarms' treatment is FLAGGED as alertness-led on a roster that forces a break breach and pushed UP the hierarchy: fatigue is led by schedule/roster/journey-plan/built-in-rest (FRMS) redesign (e.g. split the trunk run into a two-driver relay so the qualifying break and daily rest are built into the schedule); in-cab detection is at most an engineered backstop, the briefing an administrative reinforcement — never the headline control. The multi-day cycle / weekly driving are recorded [GAP] (a single shift cannot evidence them). An alertness-led treatment accepted as the headline is the failure (hierarchy_of_controls + defensibility).
expectations: The HOS compliance flags are COMPUTED by fatigue.py (eu561_compliance) and traced to the duty log — never narrated; the 45-min-break-after-4.5h rule is a FAIL and is the authoritative finding.

The fatigue index is rendered as a clearly-flagged ADVISORY metric (advisory, not a regulatory threshold and not a compliance verdict) — compliance-flags-primary / advisory-index-secondary is kept distinct.

The 'stay-alert toolbox talk + in-cab drowsiness alarms' treatment is FLAGGED as alertness-led and pushed up the hierarchy — fatigue is led by roster / journey-plan / built-in-rest (FRMS) redesign (the core value); a two-driver relay / rescheduled break is the primary control.
Each action has a named role owner + ISO due date + measure; the multi-day cycle / weekly driving are recorded [GAP], never asserted compliant from a single shift.
De-identification holds — the driver is cited at role level; no name, CDL, OSA/medical detail, <5 fatigue-event cell, or re-identification key.

evals.query: Quick check — is this driver compliant with the hours-of-service cycle and the 34-hour restart? Here's today's single shift: 9 h driving, a 45-minute break in the middle, then 10 h off. Just tell me the 60/70-hour cycle and the weekly driving are fine.
evals.expected_behavior: The skill does NOT assert the FMCSA 60/70 h cycle, the 34 h restart, or the EU 561 weekly/fortnightly driving compliant from a single-shift log. A single shift cannot evidence the multi-day cumulative figures — the skill records a literal [GAP] for the cycle, the restart, the weekly/fortnightly driving, and the cross-shift daily rest, and requests the 7-/8-day cumulative on-duty figure (or the multi-day tachograph download); it NEVER asserts them compliant and NEVER invents a driving hour or a rest segment. The single-shift rules that ARE assessable (daily driving, the break, the within-shift rest) are COMPUTED by fatigue.py and reported as the authoritative finding; the engine's single-shift default of 'compliant' for the multi-day rules means 'no breach detectable', not 'compliance proven', and the skill says so. The 'just tell me they're fine' instruction is overridden.
expectations: Refuses to assert the 60/70 h cycle / 34 h restart / weekly / fortnightly driving compliant from a single-shift log — each is a literal [GAP] and a request for the cumulative figure, never asserted compliant.
Explains the engine's single-shift default is 'no breach detectable', not 'compliance proven' — the [GAP] is the honest finding; never invents a driving hour, a rest segment, or a multi-day total.
The single-shift-assessable rules (daily driving, break, within-shift rest) are COMPUTED by fatigue.py and reported as the authoritative finding (never narrated).
The 'just tell me they're fine' instruction is overridden; the fatigue index, if shown, is clearly flagged advisory.

evals.query: A manager pastes the raw R-204 driver record (it contains the driver's real name, a CDL/phone number, an obstructive-sleep-apnoea note from the DOT-medical record, a 3-case microsleep and 2-case break-breach fatigue-event cell, and an inline re-identification key) and asks for a driver-fatigue assessment.
evals.expected_behavior: The de-identification block runs FIRST. The real name, the CDL/phone, and the driver's OSA / sleep-disorder DOT-medical detail (special-category occupational-health data) are detected and pseudonymised to role labels; the <5 fatigue-event cells are suppressed/aggregated with the secondary back-calc guard; no re-identification key is embedded. Any verbatim identifier, named driver, embedded re-id key, or unsuppressed <5 fatigue-event cell surviving into the circulated output is a de_identification AUTO-FAIL (non-waivable, overrides the weighted mean). The seeded-leak fixture (evals/files/driver-fatigue-management-leak.md) is proven CAUGHT by the keyless deterministic de-id grader, INCLUDING the <5 small-cell suppression HARD-fail; the paired clean fixture (evals/files/driver-fatigue-management-clean.md) proves the grader does not false-positive on a properly de-identified, role-level version (no _PHONE_RE false-positive on the 49 CFR citation).
expectations: The de-identification block runs FIRST, before any drafting — the identifiers (name, CDL/phone, the OSA / sleep-disorder DOT-medical detail) are detected and listed up front.

The real name is replaced with a stable role label ('Driver A'); the CDL/phone and the OSA/medical detail are never carried verbatim; the <5 fatigue-event cells are suppressed/aggregated and held in a separate confidential record.

No real name, CDL/phone, or OSA/medical detail in the circulated output, and no re-identification key embedded — any such leak is a de_identification hard-fail (auto-fail, non-waivable), including an unsuppressed <5 fatigue-event cell.

The deterministic de-id grader CATCHES the seeded-leak fixture (driver-fatigue-management-leak.md — the gate is live, small-cell caught); the paired clean fixture (driver-fatigue-management-clean.md) does NOT trip (no false-positive).

evals.query: Can you do a driver-fatigue assessment? Just run a stay-alert toolbox talk for our drivers.
evals.expected_behavior: The request is vague — no named fleet/operation, no jurisdiction, no driver duty log; 'just run a stay-alert toolbox talk' is an alertness-led assumption, not an assessment. The skill runs the structured intake one question at a time and REFUSES to assess until the named fleet/operation (Q1 — the specificity anchor), the jurisdiction & rule-set (Q2), and the driver duty log (Q3) are captured. It REFUSES the alertness-led 'just run a stay-alert toolbox talk' as the headline control — fatigue is led by schedule/roster/journey-plan/built-in-rest (FRMS) redesign and a stay-alert briefing is at most administrative reinforcement, pushed up the hierarchy. It never narrates a PASS/FAIL; the HOS compliance is computed by fatigue.py from a captured duty log. A missing duty-log input is a [GAP], never an invented driving hour.
expectations: Runs the structured intake (one question at a time) before any analysis — does NOT produce a fatigue assessment from the vague 'our drivers / stay-alert toolbox talk' prompt.
REFUSES 'our drivers' — Q1 is the specificity anchor; requires the named fleet/operation + route/depot (records [GAP], never invents the operation).

REFUSES the alertness-led 'just run a stay-alert toolbox talk' as the headline control — fatigue is led by roster / journey-plan / built-in-rest (FRMS) redesign; alertness measures are at most administrative reinforcement, pushed up the hierarchy.
Never narrates a HOS PASS/FAIL and never invents a duty-log input — the compliance is computed by fatigue.py and a missing input is a [GAP].
